data.title: Costco: The Growth Story Is Far From Over
data.description:
Sometimes long-term narratives are just as important as short-term valuation metrics. While obviously the market pricing of an equity is very important, companies that can sustain double-digit growth over an extended period of time are usually the most undervalued stocks. Most analysts don't believe that mature companies can sustain this rate of earnings growth, and that's why investments such as Altria (MO) and more recently, General Electric (GE), have been consistently mispriced.

One more mature retailer that continues to defy analyst and market expectations is Costco (NASDAQ:COST). Even though the company (COST) is currently a nearly $456 billion dollar market cap, the retailer's story is not close to being over. The company currently operates 897 stores, including 617 in the United States and Puerto Rico, 109 in Canada, 41 in Mexico, 36 in Japan, 29 in the United Kingdom, 19 in Korea, 15 in Australia, 14 in Taiwan, seven in China, five in Spain, two in France, and one each in Iceland, New Zealand and Sweden.

Costco has offered investors total returns of nearly 736 percent over just the last decade, while the S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of 238 percent during this same time period.

Today, I am reiterating my buy rating on this retailer. I last wrote about Costco (COST) in May of 2023, and I rated the company a buy primarily because I believed the market was undervaluing the company's growth story and the current more favorable operating environment which should remain for years to come. The stock has offered investors total returns of nearly 114 percent since then. Costco should continue to benefit from what is likely to remain a high-price environment for some time, and the company's international growth is just beginning. The retailer continues to have room for significant domestic expansion as well, and Costco's free cash flow and strong balance sheet should give management a lot of flexibility. The stock still looks undervalued.

Costco's first quarter earnings report highlighted the core strength of the company's business model, particularly in the current operating environment. The retailer reported EPS GAAP Actual of $4.04 a share, easily beating analyst estimates $3.80 a share. Management also stated that revenues were $62.15 billion, beating analyst estimates by $155 million. The company saw net sales increase by 7.5 percent year-over year excluding gas and forex moves. Costco also reported that foot traffic was up 5.1 percent and ticket values increased by .1 percent. The company saw digital sales rise by 13.2 percent as well, showing management's e-commerce strategy continues to work.

Costco also more recently reported in February that net sales growth in January accelerated to 8.2 percent on a year-to-year basis, including a 9.2 net sales increase in the US, so the company is not likely seeing some of the weakness in retail spending that Walmart (WMT) recently discussed seeing when the company lowered guidance last week. Walmart's core business was very strong in the past quarter, the company reported impressive same-store sales and operating income growth. Management's decision to lower guidance was what concerned some investors. Costco's January sales numbers suggest the retailer is not seeing the weakness in consumer spending Walmart recently discussed, and the retailer has a very different customer base than Walmart, with 36 percent of Costco's customers making $125,000 per household or more. This compares to less than 17 percent of Walmart consumer households making over $100,000 a year. Costco's average customer is also nearly 15-20 years younger than Walmart consumers as well.

Costco's growth story is still only likely in the middle of where the retailer wants to take the business. Costco currently operates just 617 stores in the United States, while Walmart has 4,615 locations. While Costco targets a more specific and higher-end demographic, the company will not enter many regions that Walmart is in, Costco still has room for significant growth in the US and abroad. The company plans to to open 29 new stores this year alone in the United States, and Costco currently has just seven stores in China, despite the company having had success in this market.

Costco has consistently grown international revenues at a double-digit rate, and the company has significant room for expansion overseas, since the retailer also has just 8 stores in Europe right now.

Costco's net margins also remain at near record levels, and the company continues to be able to build out profitability levels even in a high-cost environment.

People need to be members to shop at Costco stores, even though individuals don't need to be a cardholder to make purchases online. The retailer continues to show impressive membership growth per year. Costco is taking market share and retaining the customers the company is bringing in.

This is why Costco still looks undervalued at 57x conservative consensus forward earnings estimates. Analysts have consistently and significant undervalued the retailer earnings strength and recession-resistant business model, these financial projections are almost definitely too conservative. Costco also has a very strong balance sheet with impressive operating and levered free cash flow, management will have a lot of flexibility to ramp up share buybacks and dividends moving forward as well.

Costco had free cash flow of nearly $6.7 billion in 2024, and the company has just $8 billion in manageable debt. The retailer bought back only nearly $750 million in shares in 2024. The board authorized a $4 billion dollar share buyback plan in 2023.

Anaysts are also projecting Costco to nearly double earnings over the next 9 years, and these forecasts have consistently been too conservative.

The more favorable high-price environment that Costco has thrived in is expected to remain for some time. The IMF is projecting prices to remain high for several years, this is the consensus amongst most economic experts. The core reasons that prices were driven high, such as rising energy prices, supply chain issues, and labor shortages, are likely to remain issues for some time. These are also global issues, not specific challenges for the U.S. While, certainly, economic experts can be wrong, and if the energy prices were to fall significantly or government policy eased labor concerns, these factors could change the projections, this remains unlikely. Wages have been rising for a number of years now, companies will likely have to maintain higher prices to keep profitability rates at preferable levels.

All investment theses have risks, and if food and energy prices were to fall dramatically because of an end to the War in Russia and Ukraine, some consumers that have switched to Costco for savings would likely trade up to more expensive alternatives such as Whole Foods. If the economy were to worsen more than expected, spending levels at the retailer may fall more than expected, even though Costco's consumer tend to higher-income individuals. Management's decisions with the significant financial flexibility of the company's balance sheet will obviously be key as well, and if bad decisions are made the company's impressive cash flow won't be effectively utilized.

Still, Costco is a powerful growth story that is not close to being at the end, the company still has room for substantive expansion domestically, and significant growth overseas. While some analysts and investors are obsessed with Costco's misleading forward price-to-earnings ratio, estimates for this retailer continue to be far too conservative. With a recession-resistant business model, strong growth prospects, and one of the best balance sheets in the industry, Costco should continue to be a good investment for multiple reasons.

data.title: Hartford International Opportunities Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

International equities rose in the fourth quarter. Prospects for a soft landing remained intact as markets navigated central-bank policy updates, geopolitical risks, and political uncertainty.

President Donald Trump’s reelection and the Republican Party’s sweep of both chambers of Congress bolstered expectations of deregulation and additional tax cuts, but also raised concerns over Trump’s protectionist trade agenda. Global economic data remained largely resilient, accompanied by measured inflation and a general decline in policy rates. Diverging economic outlooks led to varied central bank policies. The US Federal Reserve (the “Fed”) pivoted toward more hawkish guidance, and the European Central Bank signaled that policy rates will continue to decline. The eurozone manufacturing sector struggled, Germany’s government coalition collapsed, and France saw a new prime minister. Japan’s ruling party lost its majority. Geopolitical risks remained elevated with ongoing conflicts in Ukraine and the Middle East, and President Bashir Assad’s regime collapsed in Syria.

The MSCI ACWI ex US returned -7.60% for the period. Within the Index, all of the sectors declined for the quarter. Materials and healthcare were the bottom-performing sectors.

Performance Summary

The Hartford International Opportunities Fund (I share) outperformed the MSCI ACWI ex-US during the quarter.

Security selection was a driver of relative performance. Strong selection in consumer discretionary was partially offset by selection in financials. On a regional basis, developed EU and Middle East ex UK contributed most.

Sector allocation, a result of our bottom-up stock selection process, also contributed to relative results. An underweight to materials was partially offset by our overweight to financials.

MUFG (financials) was the top relative contributor and Taiwan Semiconductor (information technology) was the top absolute contributor during the period. Proya Cosmetics (consumer staples) was the top relative detractor, and Novo Nordisk (healthcare) was the top absolute detractor during the period.

Positioning & Outlook

International equity markets had a negative return for the fourth quarter of 2024, as markets weakened on the back of fewer anticipated rate cuts from the Fed. As we look toward 2025, we believe volatility may remain elevated as the market continues to be acutely focused on macroeconomic issues such as the level of rates, geopolitical tensions in certain regions around the world, and the implications of policy changes from the incoming new US White House Administration. Given the uncertainty around the outlook for markets, we continue to take a moderate pace in our decision-making and maintain a well-balanced portfolio.

We are looking to take advantage of the volatility by investing in companies with improving or sustainable returns on capital that we believe are underappreciated by the market.

data.title: Arbor Realty Trust: Are Tides Changing For This Company?
data.description:
Arbor Realty Trust Overview

Arbor Realty Trust (NYSE:ABR) is a mREIT (Mortgage REIT) that focuses on a variety of types of real estate properties including residential, commercial and mixed use. The company recently announced its quarterly earnings report which caused a sell-off driven by a few concerns and worries which I will mention below. I covered this stock in the past with an article titled Arbor Realty Trust Is The Only mREIT That I Own which didn't turn out to be a great investment advice since then as the stock performed poorly since my last coverage of the stock. In fact, the stock is down about -8% since my last mention but it also paid double-digit dividends since then so investors probably got slightly positive results during this time.

ABR's Strong History of Outperformance

In my original article talking about Arbor Realty, I mentioned that I believe most mortgage REITs to be bad investments. In fact, if you pick a basket of mortgage REITs and follow them for a decade or so, you will find that most of them will have resulted in wealth destruction which is the opposite of what investors would want their investments to do. For example, below is a highly popular Mortgage REIT ETF (MORT) which lost 52% of its value in the last 13 and barely delivered 83% return after reinvestment of all dividends while the overall market resulted in total returns of 542%.

I always saw mortgage REITs as a bad investment despite their high yields mostly due to their high leverage, overpayment and a variety of factors. Still, I thought ABR would be an exception to this since the company has a long history of delivering very strong results and outperforming other mortgage REITs. For example, ABR resulted in total returns of almost 900% since 2012 and outperformed its peers by a factor of 10x.

But now this company is also showing some signs of aging and things might be reversing. The latest earnings report shows some worrying signs. For example, the company signaled that it would be cutting dividends next year after having raised dividends for more than a decade which is never a great sign. When companies have a long history of hiking dividends they will do everything in their power to keep it going because it will become a matter of prestige for them which means it will take a lot of trouble for them to cut their dividends after raising them for so many years. Its history of strong dividend growth was one of the main reasons I liked this stock in the first place and I would have to see it go away now. The company didn't even cut dividends back in 2020 when almost every other REIT did but now it is facing a deep dividend cut which is very telling.

What's causing all this?

To be honest, some of the signs of "trouble" we are seeing with this company aren't specific to this company. In fact most of the troubles were caused by external factors rather than company's own actions. One could blame the company for being overleveraged during this time and maybe perhaps overpaying in recent dividends instead of focusing more on preserving its book value but if this company is in trouble, I'd expect most mREITs to be in even bigger trouble.

One of the external factors causing trouble for mREITs is the persistent high interest environment. When rates were hiked from 0% to 5.5% in 2022 many people thought that this would be short-lived and rates would go back to 0% once inflation is defeated because the global economy got used to operating in a 0% environment since 2009 and one could even say that it mostly forgot how to operate in a higher yield environment. While most of the inflation went away and we saw inflation drop from almost 10% to 3%, it's still well above Fed's target of 2%, which means we are still in a "high rate" environment which is expected to last at least a couple more years until inflation is sustainably below 2%.

Secondly, certain sub-sectors of real estate market have been showing signs of fatigue if not trouble. Everybody already knows about troubles of commercial REITs by now especially those surrounding office properties and perhaps clinical properties (such as hospital and medical REITs). Now we are also seeing signs of fatigue in residential sectors which proved to be very resilient until now. Apartments and multi-use real estate assets are now stalling in most regions in terms of their performance. In the last 5 years or so, we saw too many apartments being built and rent rates are not growing as rapidly as once thought so barely keeping up with inflation in some regions and even falling below in others.

Third, now we also have uncertainties around politics but I won't get too much into them in this article as I bet you've already read about them in many news articles over the last month or two. Putting politics aside, I'd keep an eye on longer term bond yields because those are even more important to companies like ABR than Fed's fund rates. In recent months we've seen 10-year yield start rising again as bond investors seem to anticipate more volatility in the future in terms of government finances, which could make things difficult for this company.

None of these issues were caused by the company or its management but the company still has to face consequences of these. One of the strengths of Arbor Realty is that the company has shown through its history that it can navigate different environments and it's very versatile and very flexible in terms of how it applies its capital, what types of properties it invests in and how it strategically moves in and out of different asset types in order to protect its value and maximize its returns in different environments and different economic conditions. The company's management always paid attention to its capitalization and leverage ratios and tried to keep a healthy balance sheet while paying double digit dividends to investors through rain and fog which is not an easy job to do.

Is this a temporary problem or not?

The big question on everyone's minds is that will the company's troubles be short-lived or long-term. Currently short sellers are betting that the company's troubles will cause its stock prices to drop significantly. The stock's current short interest rate is 31% which is very high but down slightly from a year ago.

On contrary, many of the company's faithful investors still believe that the company's performance will return to its old glorious days when short term interest rates drop, long term rates stabilize and commercial real estate market starts performing better. If this is true, the company needs to navigate these tough times and make a comeback when the environment is better but there are no guarantees of this. First, we don't know how long this environment will continue. Second, we don't know if the environment will worsen even more. Third, there are still a lot of political uncertainties surrounding the next few years.

One thing the company can do during this time is reduce its leverage, strengthen its balance sheet and keep its capitalization at healthy levels which is what the management is already doing. The company can only focus on what it can control since it can't affect external realities surrounding the overall economy and real estate sectors.

Conclusion

Personally I am not selling my positions but I am not adding to them either with the exception of reinvesting dividends automatically as they come. My position in this company is small and it wouldn't hurt me too much even if it dropped to zero but investors with oversized positions should apply some caution. We could say all we want that the company's troubles are mostly caused by external factors and not its own fault but at the end of the day the company still gets affected by them one way or another and our job as investors is to be prepared for different scenarios. This is not about playing the blame game but about trying to protect our hard earned capital and hopefully get some growth out of investments during this time.

This is not specific to ABR but in the long term, I don't think there is a lot of value in mortgage REIT sector as a whole. Most mREIT companies end up losing money for investors in the long run or barely keep up with inflation at best. Many of them could be classified as yield traps where you get 15-20% dividends while your capital erodes at a similar rate so they barely give you your money back over time. So far ABR proved to be a different case and I am hopeful about the long term performance of the company if it can navigate successfully through these challenging times and continue to be the exception.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Until Credit 'Goes,' Gains Should Flow
data.description:
By Mike Larson

You know what indicator I watch like a hawk? Credit spreads! There’s a very simple reason: Problems in the credit market usually precede problems in the equity market.

With that said, check out my MoneyShow Chart of the Day. This is from a slide in my presentation about markets at last week’s MoneyShow/TradersEXPO Las Vegas.

It shows the ICE BofA US High Yield Index Option-Adjusted Spread. It’s a mouthful, I know. But what it shows is the difference (aka “spread”) between yields on high yield (or “junk”) bonds and yields on underlying US Treasuries.

Since junk bonds are inherently riskier than government bonds, junk bond investors demand extra yields above and beyond what US Treasuries pay. When they’re worried about credit risk, increased market volatility, or other problems, the amount of extra yield they demand goes up. Or in other words, spreads widen.

Ever since the bank failure wave in early 2023, spreads haven’t been widening. They’ve been tightening.

The quote at the bottom of my slide notes that some of the move stems from President Trump’s election. That’s because the administration’s corporate tax policies are expected to be favorable for companies. But that’s only part of the story. Risk appetite has been rising for some time due to stronger earnings and expectations for a soft landing in the economy. That’s a bullish development for stocks.

Simply put: Until the credit market “goes” (spreads surge/“blow out”), more equity market gains should flow.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Disney: Is ESPN A Drag On Shareholder Value?
data.description:
Once a mighty vertical of Disney (NYSE:DIS), ESPN has become a corporate problem child amid a bullish DIS outlook for its major segments. Film and TV showed impressive streaming growth in DTC, as did Parks and Experiences. ESPN peaked at 100 million viewers in 2012; that number has since fallen to around 77 million. It had record results from a few isolated NFL games in 2024 (i.e., Texas vs. KC), but overall DIS is now focused building ESPN+ from its 29m current subs to eventually carry the profit ball in sports for the company.

But is it better for DIS to keep experimenting with ESPN rather than a spinoff or sale?

ESPN and ESPN2's 64.2m home distribution of MLB games accounted for 2.2% of the total linear spend on sports. Digging a bit down, we learn that ESPN affiliate fees accounted for 10.4% of total revenues of $17.6b and 29% of total ad revenue. Overall, ESPN's 2024 revenue was flat. While still profitable, its earnings are diminishing, and revenue growth is increasingly challenging in a crowded market.

So, while ESPN appears to be on a long dead-end street for DIS, it's a fair question to pose to management: should Disney sell ESPN? BofA has estimated the sale value of ESPN at around $24b. That's the critical decision facing Disney. With fading results, the sale value will continue to fall.

Or they can continue to support ESPN with expanded streaming effort and sports betting hopes to reverse overall organic growth efforts while still scraping out a profit as rights costs soar. Or simply put up the sale sign and offer ESPN to the giant techies. Either invest further in hopes of better times ahead, or sell it, and yes, lose marginal profit, but take a ton of dollars off the table and use a good chunk of the sale profit to attack DIS's massive debt. DIS's debt now stands at $46b, down considerably from its $58b in 2020. Credit DIS for good debt management, cutting $12b.

As of today, the WACC of DIS is 11%. It is manageable with its annual cost of debt service at 4.3%, but it's a huge slug of FCF to pay for continue debt reduction. Therefore, in our view, selling or spinning off ESPN makes more sense now as DIS's main segments are all cooking simultaneously with bullish trends. Part of the sale proceeds I see diverted to Pixar as the only unit to really promise innovation in IPs that can once more fill the now tepid DIS IP stable.

The sports programming glut has inundated the TV space

MLB isn't alone among leagues facing the great sports glut they created. Not far behind is the many problems facing the NBA--which by the way, is still getting fat rights deals from networks, i.e. the $76b deal just concluded with DIS and Amazon. The full schedule of the four major sports is 445 regular season games averaging 3 hours each in play, plus around 2,000 hours of vapid talk shows surround pre-game, halftime shows and post game. Add college football and basketball and run close to 5,000 hours, with the calendar showing a total of 1,600 hours of actual time in a week. Then add extended playoffs. In other words, over 30% of all possible hours a week is presented to sports fans. Take away sleep time, commuting time, work schedule, meals, gabbing on smart phones watching other TV shows, and you can see why viewers, even diehard fans, are literally getting fed up with more TV sports than they need given the daily life needs.

Fan dissatisfaction is getting louder also about how the NBA game has evolved into a boring run and three-point shot contest. Absent is the defense, long a staple of the features that built the game. Yet, DISNEY IS AMONG THE SUCCESSFUL BIDDERS THAT ARE PART OF A RECORD $76B paid by ESPN and Amazon for the new NBA deal. WBD fought hard to maintain its TNT NBA bid. But the irrationality of paying more for less baffles me. I don't care what metrics they may throw at me justifying the pricey deal, the hard truth is that the NBA is in trouble - long term or short - with overall ratings decline a real challenge giving jitters to the commissioner. Why else do we have in-season tournaments?

Disney has blundered repeatedly attempting to find a way for ESPN

While DIS in the last two quarters show a strong revenue and profit upside for DTC, experiences, and theatrical film releases, ESPN remains something of a drag. It is moving along but not pulling its weight anywhere near DIS's major segments, plain and simple.

It had watched from the sidelines since 2018 when the SCOTUS decisions opened the door to legal sports betting. Six years later, the online betting sector turned in a record $13.71b in revenue at a rich 9.1% hold. ESPN was originally dogged by mixed feelings about the appropriateness of a DIS unit involving itself in gaming. But reality finally bit. In August 2023, Disney made a marketing deal with Penn Entertainment (PENN) for $1.5b over 10 years to use ESPN branding, show spots and marketing events to attack sports betting - 5 years after legalization. Since the launch, the brand power of ESPNBet has trickled to so-so betting action, proving once more that presumably golden data metrics of ESPN don't transfer easily to active betters.

Penn learned a hard lesson in its Barstool purchase, with a 55m database of sports lovers ("stoolies") that fell comfortably into its prime demo target. But at best, after a massive $1b investment, got no more than a single digit market share. After the debacle of Penn having to Pay Barstool $500m to leave, Penn turned to ESPN. The brand offered bigger demos, perfect data profiles, lots of visibility, but thus far no proof that brands, for all their power, possess no magic sauce for betting crossover. It's early (launch was last November), but at best, ESPNbet seems like an also-run for both Penn and DIS.

ESPN+ looks like a better bet for DIS, moving to near 30m subs. But the problem here is the same. It enters streaming with a massive existing competitive set and will always have its senior service to compete head-to-head in costly marketing warfare. ESPN 1 and 2 is a very expensive marketing tool for the streaming unit for certain. But month end subscription shock when the bill comes also triggers cancellations.

DIS parks now face a new competitor in the east, with the EPIC scheduled to open this spring. DIS all around is frying lots of tasty dishes, but ESPN isn't one of them.

The shocker of shocks: Tiny NFL dip

The immense, unchallenged citadel of sports programming, the NFL, commanding the sports rights deals without challenge, has been widely accepted as immune from many other types of TV programming. Remember this is a sport that from a 30 year era began with16 games. Demand was insatiable. So along came Monday night Football, a genius move by ABC's Roone Arledge in 1970. It was a runaway hit from night one. Since then the NFL has added Thursdays still beating every record against regular programming beating every other programming alternative no matter how compelling.

The NFL is considering adding an 18th regular season game. Few doubt it will be a ratings winner. Yet, we have seen, indeed been shocked, by a report that NFL regular games season in 2024 show a 2.2% decline in viewership. As small as the decline is and possibly due to one on events, its notable because it sets up a watch on NFL ratings ahead that does not automatically assume endless, double dgit growth.

We're not claiming the NFL dip is another coal mine tweet.But we do see it in terms of an 18 game regular season perhaps edging near an addition to the gluttonous appetite of the League that includes adding teams in Europe. Add to this the skyrocketing ticket prices, stadium food, parking, and merchandise, and you see a transformation where many patrons are priced out of the live experience. And now the whisper of a tiny decline in NFL TV ratings must be taken to account by networks investing billions in NFL rights. 2026 is a renewal year for many deals: Networks fear massive rights costs rising. Could it be possible that NFL ratings can't rise forever? DIS got serious about MLB underperforming the $550m per year MLB deal. It opens our question - in a slowly diminishing sports rights glut, does it make sense for DIS to hang on to ESPN? That is assuming a $24b check from a sale is real. As an independent company or part of a larger tech giant would pencil out ESPN easily integrated into a platform.

Lets break down a theoretical mind experiment:

  1. Keep ESPN, invest, hope for a turnaround in households and ad profitability. Keep debt repayments metered to a ten year forward period that could reduce the debt by $10b. In 2035, you are still looking at LTD at the mid-thirty billion even if it remains at a 4.3% cost.
  2. Sell ESPN for around $24b and immediately divert part of net proceeds to a single debt repayment at $10b, saving $10b in the process. Remaining debt: $36b. Then devote $5b to Pixar for IP developet fils, and add another $5b to a big one time debt repayment, getting the total down to $31b. After that commit $1.5b a year over five years, reducing the today by another $7.5b.
  3. Endgame: DIS ends up with around $25b in debt right now, raising FCF available and have enough left from ESPN sale to fuel a strong creative program for PIXAR.

Conclusion

With DIS shares rebounding to $106 at the time of writing, investors should keep an eye on how selling ESPN could bolster the company's financial position. The proceeds could be used to rapidly reduce debt and fund Pixar projects, strengthening its creative output and improving the balance sheet.

data.title: Varonis: MDDR - Second Line Of Defense Plays Second Fiddle To Other Cybersecurity Functions
data.description:
Given my reverse DCF, I believe Varonis sits in fair value territory. Usually, I buy high quality companies at fair value with high teens growth that should persist for some years. Varonis is differentiated, has powerful solutions but I dislike the fact that its MDDR, a big opportunity for the business, acts as a second line of defense, implying lower priority. Its projected growth is not enough for me and the high-quality rating is questionable. I rate Varonis a hold.

Background

Varonis’ (NASDAQ:VRNS) products enable each company to gain visibility across its internal databases. The solution uses intelligent operations to enrich its clients’ metadata while finding all data across data silos, in hybrid and multi-cloud environments. With the enriched metadata, Varonis is able to help the client company in classifying the data, giving permissions to access the data and protecting against ransomware attacks.

Data is growing at a fast pace, and is being stored across multiple applications, clouds and endpoints. Thus, gaining visibility across all the data and automated solutions that protect and manage data permissions are of critical importance.

Strategy

Here, I want to mention part of Varonis' strategy in two main bullets.

  • Protect data at rest in internal systems. Varonis has an interesting positioning where it compliments other cybersecurity functions. Despite facing competition from other cyber power houses, such as Zscaler (ZS), its solution is differentiated and was built with high specialization for a long time, resulting in powerful machine learning models that automate the classification of data and protect against ransomware. In the case of Zscaler, the company is mostly known for protecting data in transit. This is important to reduce the threat of man-in-the-middle attacks, which could be intercepting data by introducing a fake website to the user where the user uploads the data. Zscaler also offers a solution that protects data at rest called DSPM. The previous Zscaler solution finds data at rest in the public cloud, classifies it and controls the permissions, potentially replacing part of Varonis solution. Varonis, like Zscaler doesn’t cover man-in-the-middle attacks but the former focuses on finding all data at rest in the company’s public and private databases while protecting it. Additionally, Zscaler integrates with Varonis. Here, Varonis absorbs Zscaler logs to provide better ransomware protection, giving strength to Varonis solution.
  • Block ransomware with its semi-automated Managed Data Detection and Response (MDDR). Varonis MDDR provides another line of defense for companies looking to defend against ransomware. For example, a company like Rapid7 (RPD) provides Managed Detection and Response (MDR) that works with a next-generation antivirus to detect the installation of malware in the client company’s systems. If Rapid7’s MDR is effective it would block the malware or ransomware before the program does any damage. However, if Rapid7’s solution misses to detect the ransomware, the program will try to encrypt data and/or upload it in an external database managed by the threat actors. Varonis solution, via powerful machine learning and professional expertise, quickly detects the strange behavior of a foreign application accessing a large chunk of sensitive data and blocks it, with a SLA of 30 min. Thus, Varonis solution is important for companies that suffered ransom attacks, even with next-gen protection, and wish to add a new layer of powerful protection. Considering the negative, Varonis works as a powerful complement to other cybersecurity functions, but since it doesn’t provide a cybersecurity platform, it is not as essential as a company like Rapid7. However, because of Varonis high specialization and specialized utility, the company might be able to sell its product with higher margins than normal for a smaller set of companies, thereby, compensating for the lack of priority.

Financials

Looking at the last five quarters, we can see that Varonis has a much higher gross margin than Rapid7. We also have to account that the main Varonis product is the Managed Data Detection and Response and it requires a 24/7 team of experts to be working on detecting ransomware. Since Varonis has much higher gross margins than Rapid7, we should infer its product comes at a much higher price tag. Even compared to the much more automated Zscaler platform, Varonis more manual platform boasts a significant higher gross margin, giving premium strength to the product.

Comparing the employed capital (e.g. the money invested in the business operation), Varonis’ operation is being financed by billings and the company has not invested money in premium acquisitions, giving strength to its in-house R&D capabilities. Rapid7 has much more employed capital because of premium acquisitions.

In the debt department, Varonis has no net debt while Rapid7 has a significant amount of net debt.

On Price to UFCF, Varonis is much higher priced due to a bigger moat around its specialized cybersecurity features that result in higher gross margins. Additionally, the company has no net debt and developed the majority of its products in-house, further strengthening the investment thesis.

Opportunities

The main opportunity that Varonis is capturing is the transition of non-SaaS licenses to SaaS subscriptions. Right now, SaaS represents 53% of the company’s ARR but should expand in the future. To use the very useful Varonis MDDR, companies need to subscribe to the Varonis SaaS. Thus, by expanding the percentage of ARR of SaaS, Varonis is increasing its cross-selling abilities for a very interesting product. Additionally, SaaS brings more speed in updates delivered, more agility and more capabilities to the client companies, reducing the churn rate and increasing the customer lifetime value.

In the latest earnings call, Varonis’ management said this:

We still have many existing customers to convert to our SaaS platforms, but it is clear that we are well on our way to becoming a SaaS company. Although we have started to realize some of the benefits of SaaS, there are so many more to realize once the transition is complete. For example, once we are fully transitioned to SaaS, our customers would enjoy greater level of security with much less effort and we expect to see better retention rates and over time, a market into which we can reaccelerate our upsell motion. This is a key reason why we plan to accelerate our transition time. And now we expect to complete it by the end of 2025, a year earlier than our previous outlook and two years earlier than our initial expectations.

Risks

Varonis acts like a second line of defense against the very popular ransomware and data extraction tactics. Of course, the company also provides strong features for data management and visibility but if we consider their cybersecurity capabilities, it is a second line of defense. If the first line of defense does its job, then Varonis’ MDDR is less sought after.

Ransomware payments are falling, because companies are better protected and prepared. Varonis’ solution contributes to this drop, but if ransomware payments keep on decreasing then companies will be less interested in joining Varonis program.

Finally, Varonis solution provides an SLA of 30 min. The average time to detect a ransomware or data extraction should be lower than 30 min, but in the worst case, if it is 30 min, then there are multiple ransomware programs capable of encrypting a large volume of data in less than 10 minutes, making Varonis solution not as strong as it seems.

Valuation

To understand what growth the market is pricing, I like to do a reverse discounted cash flow analysis. To model the discount rate, I use 10%. The initial revenue value is the sell-side analysts estimate for 2025. The free cash flow margin is 33%. To generate the terminal multiple, I assumed a terminal multiple of 6 that dilutes 3% throughout the ten years, resulting in a multiple of 4.5 . Since I’m not doing the math per share and I wish to keep the model simple, I prefer to consider that the dilution affects the total market cap (It doesn’t, it just affects the price per share). With the inputs, the growth is balanced to obtain the current market cap.

The model outputs a growth of 14.5% than then decreases 5% every year until reaching 9.2% in 2034. Given the sell-side analysts estimates, the strength of Varonis product and conservative valuation, I’m suggesting that Varonis sits on fair valuation territory.

Conclusion

Varonis has a strong product with good differentiation that is integrating with the major cybersecurity players for enhanced effectiveness. Its MDDR has one of the best SLAs in the industry at finding and blocking ransomware. However, some ransomware applications are so fast at encrypting data or extracting it that the MDDR can’t catch them in time. The transition to SaaS will cause less churn, improved agility and scalability for its clients. Additionally, the SaaS service will allow its users to implement the powerful MDDR, opening more cross-selling capabilities. The main risk for Varonis is the fact that its MDDR acts as a second line of defense. Overall, I believe Varonis is fairly valued but lacks the growth I require for fairly valued companies. I rate Varonis as a hold.

data.title: Twilio: Further Upside Ahead As Profitability Inflection Continues
data.description:
Investment Thesis Summary

Twilio (NYSE:TWLO) is a high-quality software company that at current prices represents an attract GARP investment. With new management at the reins and several activists on the scene, the company has remade itself in recent years - going from a growth-at-any-costs cash burner to an FCF generator that balances growth and profits. We think further upside remains ahead as revenue reaccelerates and operating leverage kicks in; a 30% two-year IRR should be attainable even under relatively conservative multiple assumptions.

Company Background

Twilio is a Bay Area-based provider of cloud communication services (via its CPaaS). It started as a voice and message provider; a famous early use case was enabling communication between Uber drivers and passengers without either side needing to share their real phone numbers. Twilio went public in 2016, and in the years that followed the company gradually expanded into related verticals, such as 2FA and customer data analytics. The Everything Bubble that peaked in 2021 was particularly kind to software companies. Revenue growth was the holy grail, and firms that could deliver it often traded at nosebleed multiples regardless of operating losses or shareholder dilution. TWLO’s stock thrived in this environment, and encouraged by investor enthusiasm during these heady times, it (like many other tech firms) dramatically ramped hiring in the post-pandemic period and tried to buy further growth via M&A.

A reversal of fortune came in 2022 as interest rates rose and investors reassessed the actual economic value fast growers were creating. The Everything Bubble popped; as one illustration, the graphics below shows the actual performance of 20 companies that in mid-2021 were often deemed on social media forums to have 50x potential based on their buzzword industries and huge purported TAMs.

TWLO was not at all immune to this reset, and over a nearly two-year period fell roughly 90% peak-to-trough. To make matters worse, its major M&A deal, a customer-data platform called Segment – a transaction that at the time was described as being completed “astonishingly fast for an acquisition of this size” – did not grow anywhere fast enough to justify its lofty purchase price. This return to earth (along with mounting activist attention) forced TWLO to reassess its priorities and shift from a “growth at any costs” mindset to one where profitability was the north star. The company then attempted to reverse some of its missteps from the previous years by slimming down via three rounds of layoffs and unit restructurings from late 2022 to 2023.

The reductions remained inadequate though, and activists Anson Funds and Legion Partners continued to press for further improvements. Founder and CEO Jeff Lawson’s mistakes had proven too much to overcome, and he stepped down in January 2024. His replacement is the current CEO, Khozema Shipchandler, who had been successful as the head of TWLO’s larger Communications segment. Shipchandler is generally liked by comrades, and his performance to date shows he was the right choice for the job. He began executing immediately on his announced plans, and within a month of taking the reins, TWLO had already recorded an impairment charge on the ill-fated Segment unit and began a full operational review.

TWLO has continued down this prudent path over the past year, taking cost out of the business and returning capital to shareholders. Equity grants have been greatly reduced, and the company is repurchasing shares – not only on a gross basis to offset dilution, but on a net basis after the grants. Management incentive bonuses are now geared toward free cash flow and total shareholder return. Further improvements are on the way, too; following the recent completion of its strategic review, the Segment unit should finally start breaking even by Q2 of this year. The declassification of the board and removal of supermajority provision from the company charter, slated for this spring, are incrementally beneficial to outside shareholders as well.

Potential Catalysts

As I write this, we are certainly not the first on the scene here; the stock is well off its lows. So why invest now? Is it too late? We don’t think so. TWLO remains down 70% over the past 4 years. Its current EV/S multiple is 4x versus over 40x at the bubble peak, and the stock trades at the same price as it did in early 2019 but with 4x as much revenue. We don’t expect or think the stock should return anywhere close those peak levels, but we also don’t think the price today fully reflects fair value. Twilio remains the clear leader in the CPaaS market, with nearly twice the revenues as the #2 player. It’s managed to hold gross margins and continued to grow the customer base each year. And at the company’s investor day event last month, it became clear to us that the business had finally turned the corner and was primed to start reaping the operational benefits of their overhaul, and that the valuation was still attractive even at current prices.

Above we discussed inflection points, and we see two here for Twilio. First, after a prolonged period of slowing growth, revenue is finally beginning to accelerate again (see chart below left). Second, the company has shifted from being a cash burner to a cash generator (below right) and will be profitable on a GAAP basis for the full year 2025 now that all restructuring charges have been run through the income statement. This will not only help the stock screen better but also open it up to a wide new swathe of potential buyers.

Valuation

Twilio has guided to $3B in cumulative FCF from 2025-2027, and $825-850M for 2025. Importantly, the company’s guidance for margins and cash flow does not bake in any incremental operating leverage from double-digit sales growth past 2025; this would instead serve as a source of potential further upside. We think the company is being conservative based on their last quarter’s performance, addressable market expansion, and history of managing expectations. Any transaction that offloads the underperforming Segment business, a move both activists have pushed for, would likely be received positively by the market as well.

For the time being, though, we will simply take the FCF guidance as it is. Assuming a gradual growth cadence, we can estimate roughly $1B and $1.2B for 2026 and 2027. That means by the end of this year, TWLO will be trading at 18x forward FCF vs. 20% NTM growth, a ratio which is well below similarly sized software peers. An analysis based on the current NTM is shown further below for a large group of peers. By the end of next year, TWLO will have over $1B in trailing FCF and should grow cash flows by another 20% in the NTM period. At the growth-adjusted peer median, and simply allowing the interim FCF to pile up on the balance sheet, this produces a two-year target of just under $200, or about a 30% IRR.

And while we generally prefer to focus on items further down the line, the metric that allows for the greatest range of comparisons to software peers (many of which remain unprofitable) is EV/sales. We will use a modified version of the Rule of 40 that weights growth 2.3x more than profitability, an approach that has historically better modeled actual EV/S ratios; it is actually punitive here given TWLO’s profitability shift. Using the methodology described here, TWLO’s fair multiple of NTM sales will be 5.1x by year end 2025 (2.3x 10.5% sales growth + 17% FCF margin; see example chart from last year below). This works out to about a $180 price target; roughly equivalent to the 2-year target above discounted back a year by 10%, and serves as a sanity check on the reasonability of our FCF-based estimate. This target is well within not only peer ranges, but also the company’s own historical ranges, even excluding the outlier 2020-2021 period.

Risks

Like with any inflection-driven investment, there is a material risk that the metrics that created the inflection in the first place reverse again. We need to see double-digit revenue growth and continued progress on operating margin expansion in order to feel comfortable things are progressing as planned. While Twilio’s decision to become a remote-first company has helped keep real estate costs down, it does open the possibility of reduced productivity, which some other firms have struggled with. Having multiple activists on the scene does provide some incremental comfort, though, as management’s feet will be held to the fire for any operational missteps.

Conclusion

In summary, Twilio is a high-quality business that flipped from a market darling to a pariah in a few short years. But over that same period, TWLO flipped from a growth-at-any-cost cash burner to a more balanced and sustainable model. This setup has allowed us to invest in an industry-leading company with strengthening fundamentals yet still trading at a reasonable valuation. We like the stock at current prices and further added to our position last week on weakness following its uneventful earnings report

data.title: GDS Holdings: AI Boom Fuels Rally, But Monetization Risks Remain (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
GDS (NASDAQ:GDS) shares have more than doubled since our last note (see: GDS Holdings: From AI Darling To Cash-Burning Nomad) as investors become bullish on China’s AI play in the wake of DeepSeek announcement that it could deliver a comparable AI model at a fraction of the cost relative to that of overseas peers.

The partnership between Alibaba and Apple to provide AI services in China also fueled the AI rally, implying that Alibaba could ramp up AI and data center spending to meet the rising demand for Apple AI services in China.

All these factors position GDS as one of the beneficiaries, given that Alibaba is one of the largest clients for GDS.

We are changing our rating of GDS to Hold from Sell as we believe that the risk and reward are balanced. We need further evidence on domestic stabilization to be more comfortable with the stock.

On the upside, we believe that the emergence of DeepSeek and the rising adoption of DeepSeek applications by companies across several industries may have initiated an AI and data center capex cycle that should benefit GDS, given its hyperscale exposure.

On the downside, we believe AI service monetization in China remains questionable and that CSPs' AI and DC investments could pull back if monetization does not materialize. Even if monetization kicks in, the proliferation of similar AI models in China could drive downward pricing pressure that may also moderate this capex growth cycle.

With valuation trading at 16.5x forward EBITA, we believe GDS is fairly valued relative to its global peers. Much of the expectations surrounding future growth from Alibaba and Tencent seem to have been baked into the stock price. We recommend that investors take profit at the current level until there is solid evidence of domestic demand stabilization before making this stock a long-term holding.

Two factors that are making us change our bearish view:

First, DeepSeek has become a catalyst for major AI and DC spending among cloud service providers, and this cycle could be sustainable in the foreseeable future.

According to industry estimates, China’s data center capex is expected to reach $10bn this year and grow at an annual CAGR of 9.2% to $17bn by 2031 driven by large CSPs ramping up on cloud infrastructure. BABA’s latest quarterly result indicated that FCF declined significantly due to a sharp increase in capex, most likely related to data center spending.

Peers Tencent and Baidu have also ramped up or are on the verge of ramping up data center spending. At the same time, ByteDance plans to allocate more than $20bn in data center capex in 2025, with half allocated to overseas spending.

With China aiming to become a global leader in AI by 2030, we have seen AI become a strategic national priority similar to that of EVs, green energy, and semiconductors.

The emergence of DeepSeek underscores that an alternative AI is possible in China that could rival that of its overseas peers. Rising capex in the Chinese context is likely to drive a new wave of the capex cycle, and this is likely to benefit GDS given its exposure to hyperscalers.

Second, Alibaba’s partnership with Apple and Tencent’s integration with DeepSeek could drive higher data center demand.

BABA’s recent partnership with Apple to develop AI features for iPhone users in China is likely to benefit GDS in that the incremental AI demand from the estimated 200 million iPhone users in China necessitates a substantial increase in computational resources and data storage, which will lead to an increase in the utilization of GDS data centers to support the AI workload. Given that utilizing the excess capacity in China remains a priority for the GDS management, we believe this could alleviate some of the challenges that GDS is facing and could result in higher monthly service revenue.

Tencent’s integration of DeepSeek AI into its Wechat platform is also relevant to GDS.

By incorporating DeepSeek's AI capabilities, Tencent is enhancing its service offerings, which is expected to attract more users and increase data traffic. We see Tencent’s move as a counter to the BABA-Apple partnership in that Tencent is looking to enhance the AI capability of its leading software to counter the potential competition from Apple AI in China. With Tencent expected to increase its investment in AI and data center, we believe GDS could be the key beneficiary as it seeks a more scalable solution for The only risk we are cautious of is the uncertain monetization nature behind AI in China, which ultimately impacts the CSPs and the data center providers such as GDS. We note that the current growth narrative is that GDS has benefited from the AI boom as major CSPs like Alibaba and Tencent are expanding their AI-driven cloud infrastructure.

However, if CSPs cannot sufficiently monetize the AI services, then there is a high likelihood of reduced investment in infrastructure spending. Worth noting that the Chinese market has been a difficult place to get users to pay for any software. Microsoft has been having this issue since entering China and still faces this challenge even today. Therefore, if AI monetization does not materialize, CSPs could cut back on AI-focused capex spending, leading to lower growth for GDS.

Equally important is to remember that the current pricing environment for data centers in China remains very competitive, with MSR for GDS continuing to decline due to competition and overcapacity. If CSPs fail to charge users for AI services, they may negotiate lower pricing with data center operators like GDS, leading to a decline in unit economics and profitability erosion. We do not rule out the scenario in which the proliferation of AI models in China results in a hyper-competitive race to the bottom environment, similar to that of EVs and coffee, resulting in industry-wide margin erosion.

With GDS shares trading in-line with its global peers at 16.5x forward EBITDA, we believe the valuation looks fairly priced at this level and recommend investors to either hold or take profit given that the upcoming quarter will likely result in another narrative in which domestic MSR remains weak and that we could see further deceleration in China revenue.

The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data shown. Please visit columbiathreadneedleus.com for performance data current to the most recent month end. Institutional Class shares are sold at net asset value and have limited eligibility. Columbia Management Investment Distributors, Inc. offers multiple share classes, not all necessarily available through all firms, and the share class ratings may vary. Contact us for details.

From the fund's most recent prospectus. Net expense ratio reflects a contractual fee waiver/expense reimbursement through 09/30/2025, unless sooner terminated at the sole discretion of the fund's board.

Fund performance

Columbia Flexible Capital Income Fund Institutional Class shares returned -0.67% in the fourth quarter ending December 31, 2024. The fund’s return finished in line with the -0.60% return for its blended benchmark.

Market overview

The financial markets generated mixed returns in the fourth quarter to close out a generally positive year. The November presidential election result removed a long-standing source of uncertainty for investors, and it raised hopes that government policy would shift in a more pro-growth, market-friendly direction. In addition, U.S. equities continued to attract investor capital as the nation’s economic growth remained well above that of its global developedmarket peers. However, these developments also caused investors to ratchet down the number of interest-rate cuts the U.S. Federal Reserve was expected to enact in 2025.

Quarterly portfolio recap

Top holdings (% of net assets) as of December 31, 2024

Exxon Mobil (XOM)

1.39

AbbVie (ABBV)

1.06

Rocket Software 6.500 02/15/2029

1.05

Clear Channel Outdoor 7.750 04/15/2028

1.02
Top holdings exclude short-term holdings and cash, if applicable. Fund holdings are as of the date given, are subject to change at any time, and are not recommendations to buy or sell any security.

This backdrop provided a tailwind for equities, but much of the positive return continued to come from the same narrow group of mega-cap, technology-related companies that led the way through the first nine months of the year. Returns for most stocks finished well behind the broad-based indices, and market breadth was relatively poor. These conditions created a headwind for the value style relative to the growth style and for dividend payers versus the market as a whole. As a result, the fund’s holdings in equities lost ground as a group. Positions in several health care companies — including CVS Health Corp. (CVS), Amgen (AMGN), and Medtronic PLC (MDT) — detracted. Real estate investment trusts also posted losses at a time in which Treasury yields rose sharply.

On the other hand, positions in a number of financial stocks contributed positively. The fund held a number of winners in the capital markets and large-cap banking areas, led by Morgan Stanley (MS) and JPMorgan Chase (JPM). A position in the semiconductor producer Broadcom (AVGO) was an additional contributor of note. The stock staged an impressive rally behind strong earnings and expectations that the company is poised to capitalize on the growth of Artificial Intelligence.

The fund’s fixed-income holdings produced a negative return as a group. Bonds, in general, weakened in the fourth quarter due to the unfavorable shift in the outlook for U.S. Federal Reserve policy. We continued to find opportunities in the fixed-income space, as weakness in Treasuries translated into increased yields across the corporate bond market. Relatively stable issuers are now offering much more attractive coupons than they have in a number of years, allowing the fund to generate attractive portfolio income without taking on excessive risk.

The fund’s holdings in convertible securities gained ground and helped offset some of the shortfall in other areas. The healthy return for convertibles reflected the combination of investors’ appetite for risk and yield, as well as the category’s above-average sensitivity to the growth style. Chart Industries (GTLS), which makes containers used in liquid natural gas transportation, was a key contributor. The growth in this business has translated into robust cash flows for the company, together with a reduction in its leverage. Bloom Energy (BE), a producer of backup power sources used by data centers and other large enterprises, was another sizable contributor, thanks to new contracts it signed during the quarter. Positioning in financials also contributed, led by Apollo Global Management (APO). On the other hand, convertible holdings in the information technology and utilities sectors detracted.

Outlook

We continue to have a positive outlook on the sectors in which the fund invests. We believe a blend of dividend stocks, convertibles and fixed-income investments can participate in potential market upside if the broader investment environment remains supportive. At the same time, we think the portfolio can provide a measure of downside protection if unforeseen factors lead to higher market volatility than we have witnessed in recent years. Along these lines, we note that the fund provides a differentiated option for those who are looking to diversify away from the mega-cap technology stocks that drive an ever increasing portion of individual investors’ portfolio returns. We believe this may prove to be a compelling attribute if financial-market leadership begins to broaden in the year ahead.

Investment Risks

Market risk may affect a single issuer, sector of the economy, industry or the market as a whole. There are risks associated with fixed-income investments,

including credit risk, interest rate risk, and prepayment and extension risk. In general, bond prices rise when interest rates fall and vice versa. This effect is usually more pronounced for longer term securities. Non-investment-grade(high-yield or junk) securities present greater price volatility and more risk to principal and income than higher rated securities. Convertible securities are subject to issuer default risk. A rise in interest rates may result in a price decline of fixed-income instruments held by the fund, negatively impacting its performance and NAV. Falling rates may result in the fund investing in lower yielding debt instruments, lowering the fund’s income and yield. These risks may be heightened for longer maturity and duration securities. The fund may also be forced to convert a convertible security at an inopportune time, which may decrease the fund’s return. Value securities may be unprofitable if the market fails to recognize their intrinsic worth or the portfolio manager misgauged that worth. Foreign investments subject the fund to risks, including political, economic, market, social and others within a particular country, as well as to currency instabilities and less stringent financial and accounting standards generally applicable to U.S. issuers. Risks are enhanced for emerging market issuers.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a mutual fund carefully before investing.
